tree.heading: Summary of Clinical Practice Guideline for Hyperkalaemia

children.heading: Guideline 1.1 - Monitoring of patients at risk of Hyperkalaemia in the community
children.content_preview: We recommend that patients known to have CKD, heart failure and/or diabetes, who are at risk of hyperkalaemia, undergo regular blood monitoring at a frequency (2-4 times per year) dependent on level o

children.heading: Guideline 1.2.1 - Monitoring of patients after an episode of mild hyperkalaemia detected in the community
children.content_preview: We recommend that the serum K + is repeated within 3 days, or as soon as feasible, if an episode of mild hyperkalaemia (K + 5.5 - 5.9 mmol/l) is detected unexpectedly in the community. (1C)
ancestor_path: Guideline 1.1 - Monitoring of patients at risk of Hyperkalaemia in the community

children.heading: Guideline 1.2.2 - Monitoring of patients after an episode of moderate hyperkalaemia detected in the community
children.content_preview: We recommend that the serum K + is repeated within 1 day of an episode of moderate hyperkalaemia (K + 6.0 - 6.4 mmol/l) when detected in the community. (1C)
ancestor_path: Guideline 1.1 - Monitoring of patients at risk of Hyperkalaemia in the community

children.heading: Guideline 1.2.3 - Monitoring of patients after an episode of severe hyperkalaemia detected in the community
children.content_preview: We recommend that patients with severe hyperkalaemia (K + ≥ 6.5 mmol/l) detected in the community are admitted for immediate assessment and treatment. (1B)
ancestor_path: Guideline 1.1 - Monitoring of patients at risk of Hyperkalaemia in the community

children.heading: Guideline 2.1 - Assessment of patients prior to initiation of ACE-I or ARB
children.content_preview: We recommend that urea and electrolytes should be assessed prior to initiation of ACE-I or ARB and these drugs should be used with caution if the serum K + is > 5.0 mmol. (1A)

children.heading: Guideline 2.2 - Assessment of patients prior to initiation of Mineralocorticoid Receptor Antagonists (MRA)
children.content_preview: We suggest that initiation of MRAs should be avoided in patients with a baseline serum K + > 5.0mmol/l or eGFR < 30 ml/min. (1B)

children.heading: Guideline 2.3 - Monitoring of patients after initiation of ACE-I and ARB
children.content_preview: We recommend that urea and electrolytes should be assessed at 1 - 2 weeks after initiation of ACE-I or ARB and after every dose titration. (1A)

children.heading: Guideline 2.4 - Monitoring of patients after initiation of MRAs
children.content_preview: We recommend that urea and electrolytes should be assessed at 1 week after initiation of MRA or after dose up-titration, then monthly for the first 3 months, 3-monthly for the first year and 4-monthly

children.heading: Guideline 2.5 - Management of hyperkalaemia in patients treated with RAASi drugs
children.content_preview:
We suggest increased frequency of monitoring in patients with a serum K + between 5.5-5.9 mmol/l and consideration of dose reduction of RAASi drugs (ACE-I, ARB, MRA). (1B)

Guideline 2.6 - Management

children.heading: Guideline 2.7 - Cessation of RAASi drugs in patients with moderate or severe hyperkalaemia
children.content_preview: We recommend cessation of RAASi drugs in patients with serum K + ≥ 6 mmol/l who do not meet the criteria for treatment with patiromer or sodium zirconium cyclosilicate. (1B)

children.heading: Guideline 3.1 - Threshold for treating Hyperkalaemia in the community
children.content_preview: We recommend that interventions to lower serum potassium be instituted in patients with a serum K + ≥ 5.5 mmol/l. (1B)

children.heading: Guideline 4.1 - Indication for assessment in hospital for patients with severe hyperkalaemia detected in the community
children.content_preview: We recommend urgent hospital assessment for all patients with severe hyperkalaemia (serum K + ≥ 6.5 mmol/l) detected in the community. (1A)

children.heading: Guideline 4.2 - Indication for assessment in hospital for patients with mild-moderate hyperkalaemia detected in the community
children.content_preview: We suggest hospital assessment for acutely unwell patients with mild (serum K + 5.5 - 5.9 mmol/l) or moderate hyperkalaemia (serum K + 6.0 - 6.4 mmol/l), particularly in the presence of an acute kidne

children.heading: Guideline 5.1 - Dietary Intervention for managing Hyperkalaemia in the community
children.content_preview: We recommend that dietary strategies to modify potassium intake is instituted for patients with CKD and persistent hyperkalaemia with a serum K + > 5.5 mmol/l after non-dietary causes of hyperkalae

children.heading: Guideline 5.2 - Role of Specialist Dietician in managing Hyperkalaemia
children.content_preview: We recommend that a registered or specialist renal dietician provides expert assessment and advice on dietary strategies to modify potassium intake in patients with CKD and persistent hyperkalaemia wi

children.heading: Guideline 6.1 - Sodium bicarbonate for management of Hyperkalaemia in the community
children.content_preview: We recommend that sodium bicarbonate is used in CKD patients with a serum bicarbonate level < 22 mmol/l with or without hyperkalaemia. (1B)

children.heading: Guideline 7.1 - Use of diuretics for managing Hyperkalaemia in the community
children.content_preview: We suggest that loop diuretics may be a useful adjunct for the treatment of chronic hyperkalaemia in patients who are non-oliguric and volume replete. (2C)

children.heading: Guideline 8.1 - Calcium resonium for the management of Hyperkalaemia in the community
children.content_preview: We suggest that calcium resonium may be used as a short-term option to treat chronic hyperkalaemia in non-hospitalised patients who do not meet the criteria for Patiromer or Sodium Zirconium Cyclosili

children.heading: Guideline 9.1 - Patiromer for the management of Hyperkalaemia in the community
children.content_preview: We recommend that Patiromer is an option in the management of persistent hyperkalaemia with a confirmed serum K + ≥ 6.0 mmol/l in adults with CKD Stage 3b-5 (not on dialysis) or heart failure receivin

children.heading: Guideline 9.2 - Patiromer for the management of Hyperkalaemia
children.content_preview: We recommend that treatment with Patiromer is discontinued if RAASi therapy is stopped. (1A)

children.heading: Guideline 9.3 - Patiromer for the management of Hyperkalaemia
children.content_preview: We recommend that Patiromer is initiated in secondary care only. (1A)

children.heading: Guideline 10.1 - Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate for the management of Hyperkalaemia
children.content_preview: We recommend that Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate (SZC) is an option in adults for the management of persistent hyperkalaemia with a confirmed serum K + ≥ 6.0 mmol/l in patients with CKD Stage 3b-5 (no

children.heading: Guideline 10.2 - Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate for the management of Hyperkalaemia
children.content_preview: We recommend that treatment with Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate (SZC) in adults is discontinued if RAASi therapy is stopped. (1A)

children.heading: Guideline 10.3 - Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate for the management of Hyperkalaemia
children.content_preview:
We recommend that Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate (SZC) is started by a specialist and continued in

Primary Care. (1A)

children.heading: Guideline 11.1 - Prevention of Hyperkalaemia in the community: monitoring
children.content_preview: We recommend monitoring of renal function in patients at risk of hyperkalaemia with known CKD, heart failure, diabetes and in any patient taking RAASi medication. (1A)

children.heading: Guideline 11.2 - Prevention of Hyperkalaemia in the community: prescribing
children.content_preview: We recommend caution in prescribing trimethoprim to patients with renal impairment or those taking RAASi drugs. (1A)

children.heading: Guideline 11.3 - Prevention of Hyperkalaemia in the community: sick day rules
children.content_preview: We recommend that healthcare professionals provide advice to patients regarding the risks of AKI and hyperkalaemia during acute illness and measures to avoid these complications. (1B)

children.heading: Guideline 12.1 - Treatment Algorithm for Hyperkalaemia in the community
children.content_preview: We recommend that the treatment of hyperkalaemia in patients in the community and out-patient setting is guided by its severity and clinical condition of the patient as summarised in the treatment alg

children.heading: Guideline 13.1 - Hyperkalaemia: Clinical Assessment; History and examination
children.content_preview: We recommend that all patients presenting with hyperkalaemia undergo a comprehensive medical and drug history and clinical examination to determine the cause of hyperkalaemia. (1B)

children.heading: Guideline 13.2 - Hyperkalaemia: Clinical Assessment; NEWS
children.content_preview: We recommend that all patients with known or suspected hyperkalaemia undergo urgent clinical assessment using an early warning scoring system to assess level of acuity. (1C)

children.heading: Guideline 14.1 - Hyperkalaemia: ECG
children.content_preview: We recommend that all hospitalised patients with a serum K + level ≥ 6.0 mmol/L have an urgent 12-lead ECG (electrocardiogram) performed and assessed for changes of hyperkalaemia. (1B)

children.heading: Guideline 14.2 - Hyperkalaemia: Cardiac monitoring
children.content_preview: We recommend a minimum of continuous 3-lead ECG monitoring for all patients with a serum K + ≥ 6.5 mmol/L, patients with features of hyperkalaemia on 12-lead ECG, and in patients with a serum K + 6.0-

children.heading: Guideline 15.1 - Hyperkalaemia: Laboratory tests
children.content_preview: We recommend that a lithium heparin anti-coagulated specimen is the sample type of choice when rapid turnaround of urea and electrolytes results is required. (1B)

children.heading: Guideline 15.2 - Hyperkalaemia: Blood gas analysis
children.content_preview: We recommend that in emergencies, K + level is measured from an arterial or venous blood sample using a point-of-care blood gas analyser whilst awaiting the formal laboratory measurement. (1B)

children.heading: Guideline 15.3 - Hyperkalaemia: Pseudo-hyperkalaemia
children.content_preview: We recommend that urea and electrolytes are measured using paired lithium heparin and clotted serum samples from a large vein using gentle traction with prompt laboratory analysis if pseudo-hyperkalae

children.heading: Guideline 16.1 - Hyperkalaemia: Summary of treatment strategy
children.content_preview: We recommend that the treatment of hyperkalaemia in hospital follow a logical 5-step approach. (1B)

children.heading: Guideline 16.2a - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 1 - Protect the heart; intravenous calcium salts; dose and rate of administration
children.content_preview: We recommend that an equivalent dose (6.8 mmol) of IV calcium is given to patients with hyperkalaemia in the presence of ECG changes at a dose and rate of 30ml 10% Calcium Gluconate over 10 minutes OR

children.heading: Guideline 16.2b - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 1 - Protect the heart; intravenous calcium salts; choice guided by clinical setting
children.content_preview: We recommend that IV Calcium Chloride is the preferred calcium salt in resuscitation (cardiac arrest or peri-arrest) and IV Calcium Gluconate should be used for all other patients in the presence of E

children.heading: Guideline 16.3.1 - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 2 - Shift K + into cells; insulin-glucose infusion
children.content_preview: We recommend that insulin-glucose (10 units soluble insulin in 25g glucose) by intravenous infusion is used to treat severe hyperkalaemia (K + ≥ 6.5 mmol/l). (1B)
ancestor_path: Guideline 16.2b - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 1 - Protect the heart; intravenous calcium salts; choice guided by clinical setting

children.heading: Guideline 16.3.2 - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 2 - Shift K + into cells; insulin-glucose infusion
children.content_preview: We suggest that insulin-glucose (10 units soluble insulin in 25g glucose) by intravenous infusion is used to treat moderate hyperkalaemia (K + 6.0 - 6.4 mmol/l). (2C)
ancestor_path: Guideline 16.2b - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 1 - Protect the heart; intravenous calcium salts; choice guided by clinical setting

children.heading: Guideline 16.3.3 - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 2 - Shift K + into cells; avoiding hypoglycaemia
children.content_preview: We recommend initiation of an infusion of 10% glucose at a rate of 50ml/ hour for 5 hours (25g) following insulin-glucose treatment in patients with a pre-treatment blood glucose < 7.0 mmol/l to av
ancestor_path: Guideline 16.2b - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 1 - Protect the heart; intravenous calcium salts; choice guided by clinical setting

children.heading: Guideline 16.4.1 - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 2 - Shift K + into cells; Salbutamol
children.content_preview: We recommend nebulised salbutamol 10-20 mg is used as adjuvant therapy for severe (K + ≥ 6.5 mmol/L) hyperkalaemia. (1B)
ancestor_path: Guideline 16.2b - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 1 - Protect the heart; intravenous calcium salts; choice guided by clinical setting

children.heading: Guideline 16.4.2 - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 2 - Shift K + into cells; Salbutamol
children.content_preview: We suggest that nebulised salbutamol 10-20 mg may be used as adjuvant therapy for moderate (K + 6.0-6.4 mmol/L) hyperkalaemia. (2C)
ancestor_path: Guideline 16.2b - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 1 - Protect the heart; intravenous calcium salts; choice guided by clinical setting

children.heading: Guideline 16.4.3 - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 2 - Shift K + into cells; Salbutamol
children.content_preview: We recommend that salbutamol is not used as monotherapy in the treatment of severe hyperkalaemia. (1A)
ancestor_path: Guideline 16.2b - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 1 - Protect the heart; intravenous calcium salts; choice guided by clinical setting

children.heading: Guideline 16.5 Hyperkalaemia: STEP2 -Shift K + into cells; Sodium bicarbonate
children.content_preview: We suggest that intravenous sodium bicarbonate infusion is not used routinely for the acute treatment of hyperkalaemia. (2C)

children.heading: Guideline 16.6.1a - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 3 - Remove K + from body; Potassium binders
children.content_preview: We recommend that Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate is used in the emergency management of severe hyperkalaemia (serum K + ≥ 6.5 mmol/l). (1B)
ancestor_path: Guideline 16.5 Hyperkalaemia: STEP2 -Shift K + into cells; Sodium bicarbonate

children.heading: Guideline 16.6.1b - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 3 - Remove K + from body; Potassium binders
children.content_preview: We suggest that Sodium Zirconium Cyclosilicate is considered in the acute management of moderate hyperkalaemia (serum K + 6.0 - 6.4 mmol/l). (1B)
ancestor_path: Guideline 16.5 Hyperkalaemia: STEP2 -Shift K + into cells; Sodium bicarbonate

children.heading: Guideline 16.6.2 - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 3 - Remove K + from body; Potassium binders
children.content_preview: We suggest that Patiromer is an option for the emergency management of acute hyperkalaemia (serum K + ≥ 6.0 mmol/l). (1C)
ancestor_path: Guideline 16.5 Hyperkalaemia: STEP2 -Shift K + into cells; Sodium bicarbonate

children.heading: Guideline 16.2 - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 3 - Remove K + from body; Cation-exchange resin
children.content_preview: We recommend that calcium resonium should no longer be routinely used in the management of acute hyperkalaemia. (2B)

children.heading: Guideline 17.1.1 - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 4 - Blood monitoring; serum K +
children.content_preview: We recommend that the serum K + is monitored closely in all patients with hyperkalaemia to assess efficacy of treatment and to monitor for rebound hyperkalaemia after the initial response to treatment
ancestor_path: Guideline 16.2 - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 3 - Remove K + from body; Cation-exchange resin

children.heading: Guideline 17.1.2 - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 4 - Blood monitoring; serum K +
children.content_preview: We suggest that serum K + is assessed at least 1, 2, 4, 6 and 24 hours after identification and treatment of moderate or severe hyperkalaemia. (2C)
ancestor_path: Guideline 16.2 - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 3 - Remove K + from body; Cation-exchange resin

children.heading: Guideline 17.2 - Hyperkalaemia: STEP 4 - Blood monitoring; blood glucose
children.content_preview: We recommend that the blood glucose concentration is monitored at regular intervals (0, 30, 60, 90, 120, 180, 240, 300 and 360 minutes) after administration of insulin-glucose infusion in all patients

children.heading: Guideline 18.1 - Hyperkalaemia: Treatment in haemodialysis patients
children.content_preview: We recommend that haemodialysis patients with severe hyperkalaemia (serum K + ≥ 6.5 mmol/L) receive dialysis treatment urgently. (1A)

children.heading: Guideline 18.2 - Hyperkalaemia: Treatment in haemodialysis patients
children.content_preview: We recommend that haemodialysis patients with severe hyperkalaemia (serum K + ≥ 6.5 mmol/L) and toxic ECG changes be treated with intravenous calcium salt to reduce risk of arrhythmias even when dialy

children.heading: Guideline 18.3 - Hyperkalaemia: Treatment in haemodialysis patients
children.content_preview: We recommend that haemodialysis patients with severe hyperkalaemia (serum K + ≥ 6.5 mmol/L) be treated with standard medical therapies to lower serum potassium if dialysis is not immediately available

children.heading: Guideline 18.4 - Hyperkalaemia: Treatment in haemodialysis patients
children.content_preview: We suggest that potassium binders may be considered to reduce the risk of hyperkalaemia during the interdialytic period. (1B)

children.heading: Guideline 19.1 - Hyperkalaemia: Specialist Referral
children.content_preview: We suggest that patients with severe hyperkalaemia (serum K + ≥ 6.5 mmol/L) be referred to their local renal or critical care team for an urgent opinion, guided by the clinical scenario and its persis

children.heading: Guideline 19.2 - Hyperkalaemia: Referral to critical care services
children.content_preview: We recommend that for patients with severe hyperkalaemia, and where there is no provision of renal services on site, referral is made to the local critical care team in the first instance, guided by t

children.heading: Guideline 19.3 - Hyperkalaemia: Escalation of care
children.content_preview: We recommend that patients are referred to the critical care team by a senior member of the referring team if escalation of care is required from the outset or if the patient fails to respond to initi

children.heading: Guideline 19.4 - Hyperkalaemia: Treatment facilities - Critical care
children.content_preview: We recommend that patients with severe hyperkalaemia and problems with airway, breathing, circulation and/or conscious level, be referred to the local critical care team in the first instance. (1C)

children.heading: Guideline 19.5 - Hyperkalaemia: Treatment facilities - Ward, Enhanced Care or Critical Care area
children.content_preview: We recommend that stable patients with severe hyperkalaemia be admitted to an area with facilities for continuous cardiac monitoring which are sufficiently staffed to support clinical monitoring and t

children.heading: Guideline 20.1 - Hyperkalaemia: Transfer to renal services
children.content_preview: We suggest that transfer to renal services be considered in clinically stable patients in whom hyperkalaemia cannot be controlled (i.e. serum K + < 6.5 mmol/L) using medical measures, particularly

children.heading: Guideline 20.2 - Hyperkalaemia: Minimum standards for safe patient transfer
children.content_preview: We suggest that any inter- or intra-hospital patient transfer is coordinated by senior clinicians and follows national guidelines. (2B)

children.heading: Guideline 21.1 - Hyperkalaemia: Prevention of hyperkalaemia in hospitalised patients
children.content_preview: We recommend that the need for prescribed medication which can cause hyperkalaemia are reviewed in the context of the current illness and level of renal function both on and during hospital admission.

children.heading: Guideline 21.2 - Hyperkalaemia: Prevention of hyperkalaemia in hospitalised patients
children.content_preview: We recommend dietary strategies to modify potassium intake for hospitalised patients with moderate or severe hyperkalaemia after non-dietary causes of hyperkalaemia (constipation, acidosis and poorly

children.heading: Guideline 21.3 - Hyperkalaemia: Prevention of hyperkalaemia in hospitalised patients
children.content_preview: We recommend that community blood monitoring is arranged on discharge for all patients who have required treatment for hyperkalaemia during hospital admission. (1B)

children.heading: Guideline 21.4 - Hyperkalaemia: Prevention of hyperkalaemia in hospitalised patients
children.content_preview: We recommend that the risk of recurrence of hyperkalaemia is considered before reinstating previous medication that may have contributed to the episode. (1B)

children.heading: Guideline 22.1 - Hyperkalaemia; Algorithm in Hospital
children.content_preview: We recommend that hyperkalaemia in hospitalised patients is managed using the treatment algorithm which provides guidance on the medical therapies and the need for initiation of renal replacement ther

children.heading: Guideline 23.1 - Hyperkalaemia; Cardiac Arrest - special circumstance
children.content_preview: We recommend that hyperkalaemia is considered in all patients who have a cardiac arrest, as part of identifying and treating a reversible cause using the 4 Hs and 4 Ts approach. (1A)

children.heading: Guideline 24.1 - Hyperkalaemia; Cardiac Arrest - Resuscitation strategy in haemodialysis patients
children.content_preview: We recommend that standard ALS practice in cardiac arrest be applied to patients requiring dialysis. (1A)

children.heading: Guideline 24.2 - Hyperkalaemia; Cardiac Arrest - Defibrillation practice in haemodialysis patients
children.content_preview: We recommend disconnection from dialysis equipment prior to defibrillation unless the dialysis machine is defibrillator-proof. (1C)

children.heading: Guideline 25.1 - Cardiac Arrest: Treatment - Intravenous calcium
children.content_preview: We recommend that intravenous calcium chloride is administered if hyperkalaemia is known or suspected to be the cause of cardiac arrest. (1C)

children.heading: Guideline 25.2.1 - Cardiac Arrest: Treatment - Insulin-glucose
children.content_preview: We recommend that 10 units soluble insulin and 25g glucose is administered if hyperkalaemia is known or suspected to be the cause of cardiac arrest. (1B)

children.content_preview: We suggest 10% glucose infusion be initiated if the blood glucose is < 7.0 mmol/l at the time of cardiac arrest. (2C)

children.heading: Guideline 25.3 - Hyperkalaemia; Cardiac Arrest - Sodium bicarbonate
children.content_preview: We suggest that sodium bicarbonate is administered if hyperkalaemia is known or suspected to be the cause of cardiac arrest. (2C)

children.content_preview: We recommend that hyperkalaemia is treated urgently in patients with severe hyperkalaemia (K + ≥ 6.5 mmol/l) and in those with ECG changes suggestive of severe hyperkalaemia. (1C)

children.heading: Guideline 26.2 - Hyperkalaemia; Prevention of Cardiac Arrest in Hyperkalaemia
children.content_preview: We recommend continuous cardiac monitoring for patients with severe hyperkalaemia (K + ≥ 6.5 mmol/l) in a setting appropriate for the level of care required. (1C)

tree.heading: Impact of chronic hyperkalaemia
tree.content_preview: Hyperkalaemia is associated with interruptions to medical therapies (i.e. RAASi and MRA), increased hospitalisation, prolongation of hospital stay, increased healthcare costs, and increased mortality.
